skill.name: vercel-automation
skill.description: Automate Vercel operations through Rube MCP, including deployments, domains, DNS records, environment variables, projects, and teams.
skill.summary: Guide AI agents through Vercel workflows using Rube MCP tool discovery and OAuth-backed actions.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution and weak-crypto alerts are false positives caused by Markdown code spans, tool names, and ordinary text. The real risk is medium because the skill routes Vercel administration through a third-party MCP endpoint and includes write-capable workflows for deployments, DNS, environment variables, projects, and teams.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Vercel Operations Through Third-Party MCP
medium_findings.description: The skill requires Rube MCP and a Vercel OAuth connection, then instructs agents to create deployments, edit DNS records, change projects, and view team data. This is legitimate DevOps automation, but mistakes or overbroad authorization can affect production services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly require an external MCP connection and list write-capable Vercel operations. The behavior matches the stated purpose, so the risk is operational rather than clearly malicious.

medium_findings.title: Environment Variable Workflows May Handle Secrets
medium_findings.description: The skill guides agents to list, add, and delete Vercel environment variables, including secret or sensitive types. These actions can expose metadata, alter credentials, or break deployments if performed without user review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly documents environment variable create, list, and delete workflows. It also notes secret values are write-only, which reduces exfiltration risk but confirms sensitive configuration is in scope.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Third-Party MCP Endpoint
low_findings.description: The skill tells users to add https://rube.app/mcp as an MCP server. This is expected for the integration, but it creates a network dependency and requires users to trust the third-party MCP provider.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint is plainly present in the setup instructions. No evidence shows secret exfiltration or hidden network behavior beyond this declared connection.

low_findings.title: Command Execution Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many backticked strings as Ruby or shell execution. In context, these are Markdown code spans, fenced examples, table entries, and Rube or Vercel tool names, not executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown-only and contains no Ruby source or shell scripts. The flagged backticks format tool identifiers and parameters for documentation.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto And Reconnaissance Alerts Are Lexical False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak-cryptography and system-reconnaissance lines do not contain cryptographic operations or host discovery commands. They contain the skill description, ID extraction guidance, and an environment variable delete reference.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found no hash algorithm usage, shell command, or host reconnaissance behavior. The alerts appear to be keyword matches against ordinary documentation text.

dangerous_patterns.title: Write-Capable Production Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow includes creating deployments, changing DNS records, updating projects, and modifying environment variables. These operations should require explicit user confirmation before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The listed tool sequences explicitly include create, add, delete, update, and deployment actions. The pattern is dangerous only if executed without clear user intent.

content.value_statement: Manual Vercel administration can be slow and error-prone across deployments, domains, DNS, and environment variables. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured Rube MCP workflows for Vercel tasks.
actual_capabilities: Searches Rube MCP tools first to get current Vercel schemas.
Guides deployment listing, inspection, log review, and event review.
Supports creating preview or production deployments from Git or files.
Manages Vercel environment variables, including secret and sensitive types.
Coordinates domain, DNS record, project, and team information workflows.
Documents common parameters, pitfalls, pagination, and rate limit handling.
limitations: Requires Rube MCP availability in the AI client.
Requires an active Vercel OAuth connection with suitable account permissions.
Does not provide native Vercel API code or standalone scripts.
Production changes still need human review before execution.

use_cases.title: Inspect Failed Deployments
use_cases.description: Review recent Vercel deployments, fetch logs, and identify the deployment state or event that caused a failure.

use_cases.title: Maintain Domain Configuration
use_cases.description: Check domain status, inspect DNS records, and prepare safe updates for custom Vercel domains.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Environment Changes
use_cases.description: List project environment variables, add scoped values, and plan redeployment after configuration changes.

prompt_templates.title: List Projects
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Vercel automation skill. Search Rube MCP tools first, then list my Vercel projects and summarize names, IDs, and frameworks.

prompt_templates.title: Debug Deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Vercel automation skill to inspect the latest failed deployment for my project. Check deployment details, build logs, runtime logs, and events before recommending next steps.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare DNS Update
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Vercel automation skill to review the domain configuration for my site. List current DNS records, identify the record that needs updating, and ask for confirmation before any change.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Production Release
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Vercel automation skill to prepare a production deployment. Resolve the project ID, confirm the Git source, check required environment variables, and create a step-by-step approval plan.

output_examples.input: Show me why the latest deployment failed.
output_examples.output: A concise deployment report with status, target environment, failing build phase, relevant log excerpts summarized in plain language, and recommended fixes.

output_examples.input: Add a DNS record for my custom domain.
output_examples.output: A domain readiness summary, the proposed DNS record details, expected propagation notes, and a confirmation request before making the update.

output_examples.input: Prepare environment variables for preview deployments.
output_examples.output: A checklist of existing variables, missing keys, target environments, secret handling notes, and redeployment steps after approval.

best_practices: Search Rube MCP tools before each workflow because schemas can change.
Ask for confirmation before creating deployments, editing DNS, or changing environment variables.
Use project IDs and record IDs after resolving names to avoid modifying the wrong resource.
anti_patterns: Do not run write actions before confirming the target project, domain, and environment.
Do not print secret environment variable values in chat or logs.
Do not assume production and preview deployments share the same configuration.

faq.question: Does this skill require a Vercel API token?
faq.answer: It uses Rube MCP and Vercel OAuth through the configured connection. Users must authorize access before workflows can run.

faq.question: Can it deploy to production?
faq.answer: Yes, the documented workflow supports production deployments when the connected Vercel account has permission.

faq.question: Can it read secret environment variable values?
faq.answer: Vercel secret values are write-only after creation. The skill notes that only identifiers are returned for secret variables.

faq.question: Does it manage DNS records?
faq.answer: Yes, it documents workflows to list, create, and update Vercel DNS records for managed domains.

faq.question: Is this a standalone command-line tool?
faq.answer: No. It is a skill that guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through available Rube MCP tools.

faq.question: What should users confirm before write actions?
faq.answer: Users should confirm the project, team, domain, target environment, and exact change before any deployment or configuration update.
